artifacts.content:
Hi Danny,

Kids are finally down and Em’s on a late shift at the clinic, so I’m catching up on life admin and figured I’d actually write instead of just sending memes.

You probably heard some version from Mom, but the last few months have been rough. It kind of feels like everything piled on at once: Dad’s heart stuff at St. Anthony, the work mess at FrontRange, me snapping at the kids, and then that full‑blown panic episode at the downtown office on January 18th when Melissa was going over status in the 7th‑floor conference room. Chest locked up, ambulance ride to Denver Health, Dr. Ravi Patel in the ER telling me my heart is fine and it was “just” a severe anxiety attack. Ever since then I keep replaying Jason’s “concerns” talk from back in early October in his office and the written warning he gave me in that 9th‑floor conference room on the Denver Health mobile app project.

I’m stuck in this cycle of checking email until 1–2 a.m., then waking up at 5:30 to get a jump on Slack because I’m terrified of losing the job and not making the $2,150 mortgage. The nights I don’t do email, I’m on the couch with 3–4 IPAs, sometimes more on Fridays, trying to shut my brain off. Emily called me out after she saw I’d dropped $186 at Mile High Liquors on Colfax in one month.

Meanwhile Sophie’s got dance, Lucas has soccer, and I’m supposed to be present and playful instead of the zombie dad who yells about spilled juice on his work laptop. I keep thinking about that late‑November blowup after the 60‑hour week at the Tech Center office.

Anyway, didn’t mean to unload this much, but you’re one of the few people I can be straight with. How are things up in Fort Collins? Still surviving high schoolers?

Mike

Hey Ryan,

You asked how things were going and I gave you the “busy but fine” line, which is only half true.

Work first: since Jason’s little “we might need to revisit your role in Q1 2025” chat back in October, I’ve basically been glued to my laptop. Between that and the written warning on the Denver Health mobile app project in August, I’m running 55–60 hour weeks. I tried to push back last October with Kevin and HR (Lisa) and even brought a printout of the UCHealth cardiologist’s note from 9/29/23 saying my chest pain was stress‑related. Kevin shifted one tiny account; Lisa mostly asked if I was still “fit for duty,” which just made me more paranoid.

The anxiety hit a new level on April 9, 2024, when I had what I’m pretty sure was a panic attack in the Maple Ridge Elementary parking lot dropping Sophie for her 2nd‑grade field trip. Sat in the car 20 minutes feeling like I couldn’t breathe, then rolled into a 9 a.m. Alpine Data Systems status call 30 minutes late. Jason called later asking why I sounded “off.” Since then, any time I’m near the school or that 7th‑floor conference room downtown, my chest tightens.

Home is its own circus. Emily’s mom, Pat, was here part of May after a huge fight with Mark about his gambling debt, and before that my cousin Elena and her little guy Mateo crashed on our couch for a few months when she left an abusive relationship and was working with SafeHouse Denver. We love them, but the townhouse feels smaller every year.

On the plus side, Lucas’s Saturday soccer is back, and I’m “assistant assistant coach,” which mostly means handing out orange slices. Tiny humans yelling “GOOOOAL” is surprisingly grounding.

You still up for that guys’ weekend hike this summer, or are we all officially old now?

Mike

Personal journal – 2025-01-20

I keep thinking about how this all snowballed.

Timeline check:

– Nov 3, 2023: Dad’s minor heart attack at St. Anthony in Lakewood. Sat in the ER with Mom while Dr. Patel explained the blocked artery and stent. That’s when I started checking my own blood pressure every night at home and Googling symptoms like a maniac.

– Late June 2024: at Dad’s house in Lakewood, he tells me the prostate cancer is back and they’re talking about radiation at St. Joseph. Since then I’ve been waking up at 2–3 a.m. almost every night, mind racing.

– April 9, 2024: panic in the Maple Ridge Elementary parking lot before Sophie’s field trip. 20 minutes sitting in the car, then 30 minutes late to the 9 a.m. Alpine Data Systems status call.

– August 22, 2024: Jason’s written warning in the 9th‑floor conference room about the Denver Health mobile app deliverable and “re‑evaluating my role” if billable hours and “client‑facing demeanor” don’t improve.

– Late November 2024: 60‑hour week at the Tech Center office, came home fried, and lost it on Sophie for spilling juice on my work laptop. Slammed my hand on the kitchen table, scared both kids.

– January 18, 2025: panic episode in Melissa’s status meeting at the downtown FrontRange office, ambulance to Denver Health, Dr. Ravi Patel in the ER saying heart is fine, severe anxiety attack.

Layer on top of that 3–4 IPAs most weeknights since around Labor Day 2024, sometimes 6 on Fridays after the kids go down, and Emily confronting me Dec 2 when she saw the $186 at Mile High Liquors. No wonder I feel like I’m coming apart.

Goal: small changes. Walks. Guitar 10 minutes. Two dry nights a week, non‑negotiable.

Subject: Quick update on Dad and the chaos here

Hi Mom,

Just wanted to send a proper update instead of bits and pieces over text.

I’m glad Dad’s doing better since the stent at St. Anthony back in November 2023. That night in the ER with Dr. Patel explaining the blocked artery scared me more than I admitted. When he told us they caught it in time, I remember thinking, “He’s 65, I’m 38, and I’m already having chest pain too.” Then last summer at the Lakewood house when Dad told me the prostate cancer had come back and they were talking about radiation at St. Joseph, my sleep really went off the rails. I’ve been waking up around 2–3 a.m. most nights since then.

On top of that, work has been nonstop. Jason gave me that written warning in August about the Denver Health mobile app project, and then in early October he pulled me into his office to say there were “concerns” about my responsiveness and they might revisit my role in Q1 2025. That’s when the obsessive late‑night email checking started.

Home has been full too. Elena and Mateo staying with us January–March 2023 while she worked with SafeHouse Denver was the right thing, but it was a lot in a small townhouse. Then last May, Pat stayed May 1–21 after the big fight with Mark over his gambling debt. Again, good to help, but it felt like we never had our own space.

The kids are okay—Sophie’s into graphic novels, Lucas is still soccer‑obsessed. We’re trying to keep Sunday pancakes as a ritual no matter what.

How are you holding up with all of Dad’s appointments?

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Quick update on Dad and the chaos here

artifacts.content:
Therapy prep – things I’ve already tried / messed with

1. Meds

– March 2024: did an online psychiatry intake with MindWay Clinic from a conference room at the FrontRange Tech Center. NP Sarah Klein started me on 25 mg sertraline. Stayed on it ~3 weeks, then stopped on 3/28/24 because I freaked out about it being in my record when MetLife did the life insurance screening. Never went to the 4/12 follow‑up.

– Late August 2023: during that brutal insomnia week, I took one of Mom’s leftover 0.5 mg lorazepam from her surgery at St. Anthony—around midnight, without telling Emily. Slept, but felt guilty and scared afterward. Didn’t repeat it.

2. Therapy-ish stuff

– January 2023: signed up for a 6‑week Tuesday night (7–8 p.m.) online CBT group through Denver Behavioral Wellness. Made it to two sessions. Bailed after a breakout where a teacher named Rachel described panic attacks in front of her class—too close to my near‑panic during the December 2022 sprint review at FrontRange.

– May 15, 2022: couples session with Pastor Jim at GracePoint Community Church. He had me keep a “sin journal” for three weeks—emailing him every time I got angry, drank more than 3 beers, or felt resentful (including about Pat and Mark after that tense Memorial Day BBQ). Stopped because it felt like I was just cataloging failures.

3. Work boundaries

– October 2023: met with Kevin and Lisa at the downtown office, brought the 9/29/23 UCHealth cardiology letter saying chest pain is stress‑related. Got one small client reassigned; left feeling like HR saw me as a liability.

Need to remember: I’ve tried things. The problem is sticking with them, not that nothing works.

Event: Panic ER anniversary check-in + kid logistics

Date: 2025-10-03
Time: 3:00–6:30 p.m.
Location: Denver / Aurora
Attendees: Michael

Notes:

– 3:00–4:00 p.m.: Personal check-in – 1 year since the October 3, 2024 ER visit at Denver Health for chest pain and 140+ heart rate. Remember: bloodwork + EKG were normal, discharge coded as “panic attack.” You’ve had fewer episodes driving home on I-25 from the FrontRange office lately. Wins: more mid-day walks, fewer 6‑beer Fridays.

– 4:00–4:45 p.m.: Drive from downtown Denver office to Maple Ridge Elementary. Pick up Sophie and Lucas.

– 4:45–5:15 p.m.: Playground time if weather is decent.

– 5:15–5:45 p.m.: Homework at home (Aurora). Check Sophie’s math and reading log.

– 5:45–6:30 p.m.: Start dinner before Emily’s back from the clinic.

Reflection prompts (do in car or after kids in bed):

• Compare today to last year’s drive home on I‑25 when episodes were 3–4 times a week.
• Notice if the 7th‑floor conference room at the downtown office still spikes anxiety the way it did after Melissa’s April 17, 2024 PIP meeting.
• Check in on sleep since Dad told me in late June 2024 at the Lakewood house that the prostate cancer was back and they might start radiation at St. Joseph.

Remember: If chest tightness ramps up, use the breathing app instead of detouring to Mile High Liquors on Colfax.

Subject: Things have been rough with Em (need to vent)

Hey Javi,

I don’t really talk about this stuff with many people, but you’ve known me since the dorm days, so here goes.

Emily and I are in a weird place. She’s still upset about a lot of things that have piled up: the money stuff, my drinking, and how checked‑out I’ve been with the kids. Back in March 2023 I quietly took out a $12,000 personal loan at the Wells Fargo branch downtown to cover some credit card debt and NBA betting losses. She found the statements on 11/10/2024 and we spent that whole weekend at the house arguing about whether she could trust me with money. She threatened separate accounts if I ever hide anything like that again.

Layer on top of that her mom Pat staying with us last May 1–21 after the blow‑up with Mark over his gambling debt, plus Elena and Mateo on our couch January–March 2023 after she left her abusive ex and started working with SafeHouse Denver. It feels like our three‑bedroom Aurora townhouse has been a revolving door of crises.

Meanwhile, I’m working 50–60 hours a week, drinking 3–4 IPAs most nights since the FrontRange reorg around March 2024, sometimes 6 on Fridays. Emily confronted me on December 2 after she saw I’d spent $186 at Mile High Liquors in a month. She’s also still shaken from that night in late November when I slammed my hand on the kitchen table and yelled at Sophie over the juice spill on my work laptop after a 60‑hour week at the Tech Center office. She told me later she felt like she was “losing” me.

I love her and the kids, but right now our relationship feels like it’s held together by logistics and Sunday pancakes.

Thanks for listening, man.

Mike

metadata.subject: Things have been rough with Em (need to vent)

artifacts.content:
From: michael.delgado@frontrangelogic.com
To: daniel.delgado@gmail.com
Date: 2025-02-10
Subject: Just need to unload a bit

Hey Dan,

I’ve been meaning to write this out instead of just sending random texts.

Home is…complicated. On paper it’s just me, Emily, and the kids here in the townhouse, but it feels like we’ve had a revolving door the last couple of years. Dad living with us from October 2023 through February 2024 after his mild stroke at Littleton Adventist Hospital, driving him three times a week to the outpatient neuro clinic in Littleton, really wiped me out. Then from March to June 2024 you were in Sophie’s room so you could get to your 90‑day outpatient treatment at Aurora Recovery Center after the second DUI in Denver. I don’t resent you at all, but it added to the sense that our place was never really ours.

It’s crazy to think back to August–December 2022 when my old college roommate, Jason Morales, was renting that same room for $650 a month while he was between contracts at Lockheed Martin and on unemployment. That helped with the mortgage, but it was tight and awkward with Sophie and Lucas.

Now it’s “just” us again, but things with Emily are rough. Since early November 2024, when she found the $7,800 balance on our joint Chase Visa from work‑travel stuff and impulse Amazon crap I never mentioned, I’ve been pretty emotionally checked out. That fight in the kitchen in Aurora was brutal, and I’ve been irritable and withdrawn most evenings for about 4 months now.

Didn’t really have a point here, just needed someone who knows the whole cast of characters.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Just need to unload a bit

artifacts.content:
From: michael.delgado@frontrangelogic.com
To: ryan.thompson@gmail.com
Date: 2025-02-14
Subject: Marriage stuff I haven’t really said out loud

Ryan,

You asked how things are really going, so here it is.

I’d describe things with Emily as loving but really strained. Since early January 2025, when she found the messages with my coworker, Alyssa Grant, from the FrontRange Logic office, it’s been tense every day. On 01/06/2025 she saw the texts on my phone at our house in Lakewood and said they crossed emotional boundaries even if nothing physical happened. She’s brought up talking to a lawyer about separation if anything like that ever happens again. We’ve had multiple late‑night arguments in our bedroom and in the kitchen about whether I’m actually committed and being fully honest.

Layered on top of that, her mom, Patricia “Pat” Carlson, moved into our guest room in August 2024 after a suicide attempt and partial hospitalization at Denver Presbyterian. Since then, Emily’s been in full caregiver mode. We’re constantly figuring out who’s driving Pat to her outpatient appointments at Rocky Mountain Behavioral Health, how much to tell Sophie and Lucas, and whether I’m “supportive enough” while I’m already working 50–60 hours a week. We’ve literally had fights in the driveway because I didn’t want to cancel plans when her mom was in crisis.

Day to day we act normal for the kids, but there’s this undercurrent of resentment and fear that makes it hard to just sit in our living room in Lakewood and relax together.

Thanks for listening,
Mike

metadata.subject: Marriage stuff I haven’t really said out loud

artifacts.content:
From: michael.delgado@frontrangelogic.com
To: carter_j@jeffersonelementary.org
Date: 2024-10-05
Subject: Home tension around Sophie’s plan

Hi Ms. Carter,

I wanted to follow up after drop‑off the other day and give you a little more context.

Since Sophie’s ADHD and anxiety diagnosis at Children’s Hospital Colorado in May 2024, Emily and I have been in a long, exhausting disagreement about how to handle things at home. Emily is very strict about routine, screen time, and homework. I tend to be more lenient to avoid additional meltdowns. On 10/02/2024 we ended up in a pretty heated argument in the Jefferson Elementary parking lot after a rough morning, with Emily saying I don’t take Dr. Nguyen’s treatment plan seriously enough.

Those fights don’t just stay in the parking lot—they bleed into our evenings in the living room in Lakewood. I’d describe our relationship as loving but tense, especially around parenting decisions connected to Sophie’s diagnosis and anxiety. We’re both trying to do what’s best for her, but it’s putting real strain on our marriage and on how calm we can keep mornings before school.

I’m sharing this because I don’t want Sophie to carry the weight of our disagreements. If you notice changes in her mood or behavior around days when there have been conflicts, that may be part of what’s going on.

Thanks for all the support you’ve been giving her.

Best,
Michael Aaron Delgado

Notes to self:

- State: CO
- Zip: 80015
- Email: michael.delgado87@gmail.com
